meta.name: WPW with Atrial Fibrillation — Irregularly Irregular Wide Complex Tachycardia

patient.name: Devon Marshall
patient.chiefComplaint: Sudden onset of palpitations, lightheadedness, chest fluttering, and shortness of breath that began ~20 minutes ago at the gym. Patient describes 'my heart is beating so fast it feels like it's going to come out of my chest.'

history.hpi: 34-year-old previously healthy male was on the treadmill at his gym when he developed sudden onset of palpitations, chest fluttering, and a sensation of impending doom. He stopped, sat down, and felt lightheaded but did not lose consciousness. A bystander called 911. Patient reports two prior 'episodes' over the past two years where his heart 'raced' for about 20 minutes and self-resolved; he never sought care because each one stopped on its own. He has been told once that he has 'a short PR or something' on a sports physical EKG but doesn't remember the details. Denies fever, recent illness, drug use, caffeine excess, weight loss, or hot/cold intolerance. Drinks alcohol socially. No prescription medications. He is now anxious, alert, mildly dyspneic, with a borderline blood pressure.
pastMedical: Possible Wolff-Parkinson-White noted on a sports physical EKG approximately 5 years ago — never followed up
Two prior self-resolving episodes of 'racing heart' lasting ~20 min each, no syncope
history.lastOralIntake: Pre-workout drink (caffeine and beta-alanine) about 90 minutes ago; protein bar 2 hours ago
history.events: Sudden onset of palpitations and chest fluttering during treadmill exercise ~20 minutes prior to EMS arrival. Stopped exercising, sat down, became lightheaded. Bystander called 911.

scene.dispatch: Dispatch: 34-year-old male, complaint of rapid heart rate and lightheadedness at the gym. Conscious and breathing. Stage 1 response.
scene.location: Commercial gym, main cardio floor. Patient seated on a bench beside the treadmill he was using. Other gym members keeping a respectful distance. Adequate lighting. Front desk staff has cleared a path.
scene.safety: Scene safe. Gym staff cooperative. Adequate room to work and to bring in cot.
scene.bystanders: Gym manager and two members. Bystander who called 911 reports patient was on treadmill, suddenly stopped, sat down, and looked pale. No witnessed loss of consciousness. No fall.
visualCues: Athletic male in workout clothes seated on bench, leaning forward with hands on knees
Pale and slightly diaphoretic; chest visibly rising and falling rapidly
Water bottle on the floor beside him
Smartwatch on wrist showing a heart-rate alert at 220+ bpm
Apparently fit, no obvious chronic illness signs

phases.name: Initial Contact
phases.description: EMS arrives to find a young, athletic, but visibly distressed male seated on a gym bench. He is alert, pale, mildly diaphoretic, and tachypneic. Radial pulse is rapid and irregular and difficult to count manually. The teaching point begins now: a previously healthy young patient with an irregularly irregular wide complex tachycardia must trigger immediate consideration of pre-excited atrial fibrillation (WPW with AFib) and the rule against AV nodal blockers.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Rapid, irregular, weak radial pulse difficult to count manually; cool/clammy skin; cap refill 2–3 sec
otherFindings: Radial pulse rapid and irregularly irregular — cannot count by palpation
Patient anxious, oriented, in obvious distress
No chest pain at this point but reports 'a fluttering' in the chest
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: My heart is going crazy. I feel like I'm going to pass out. Please help me.

expectedActions.shortAction: Primary survey; manage airway; titrate O2 to 94–98% saturation
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Adult Management steps 1–2 — manage airway, oxygen targeted to 94–98%.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Adult Management: Manage airway as necessary. Administer oxygen as appropriate with a target of achieving 94–98% saturation.

expectedActions.shortAction: Cardiac monitor; defib pads on; 12-lead EKG immediately
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia step 3 — monitor and 12-lead are mandatory; this patient needs morphology assessment.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Initiate monitoring and perform 12-lead EKG.

expectedActions.shortAction: Establish IV access (large-bore proximal site)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia step 4 — IV access required for medication administration.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Establish IV access.

expectedActions.shortAction: Check blood glucose — rule out hypoglycemia as a tachycardia driver
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia step 5 — check glucose and treat hypoglycemia per the Hypoglycemia Guideline.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Check blood glucose and treat hypoglycemia per the Hypoglycemia Guideline.

expectedActions.shortAction: Classify rhythm: irregularly irregular + wide QRS = pre-excited AFib (WPW) until proven otherwise
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Notes #3 + Patient Safety #4 — irregular wide complex must be considered pre-excited AFib; AV nodal blockers contraindicated.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: A wide-complex irregular rhythm should be considered pre-excited A-FIB; extreme care must be taken in these patients. Characteristic EKG findings include a short PR interval and, in some cases, a delta wave. Avoid AV nodal blocking agents such as adenosine, calcium channel blockers, digoxin, and possibly beta-blockers in patients with pre-excitation A-FIB (e.g., Wolff-Parkinson-White Syndrome, Lown-Ganong-Levine Syndrome) because these drugs may cause a paradoxical increase in the ventricular response.

expectedActions.shortAction: Determine stability: AMS / chest pain / SOB / hypotension / shock?
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Patient Presentation — stability determines pharmacology vs. cardioversion.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patients will manifest elevated heart rate for age and may or may not also present with associated signs or symptoms such as palpitations, dyspnea, chest pain, syncope/near-syncope, hemodynamic compromise, altered mental status, or other signs of end organ malperfusion.

transitions.condition: Crew identifies irregularly irregular wide complex tachycardia as pre-excited AFib (WPW); patient is symptomatic but mentation intact, SBP > 90; correctly avoids AV nodal blockers and selects procainamide.

transitions.if: Adenosine or diltiazem given (AV nodal blocker)
transitions.condition: Crew misidentifies the rhythm as SVT or AFib with aberrancy and gives an AV nodal blocker (adenosine or diltiazem). Block of the AV node forces conduction down the accessory pathway, ventricular response accelerates, and rhythm degenerates into ventricular fibrillation.
Patient deteriorates with progressive hypotension, altered mental status, or chest pain before pharmacology can take effect; synchronized cardioversion indicated.

phases.name: Procainamide for stable pre-excited AFib
phases.description: Crew correctly identifies pre-excited AFib (WPW with AFib) and avoids AV nodal blockers. Patient is hemodynamically borderline-stable (SBP 92, mentation intact, no ischemic chest pain) — pharmacologic conversion with procainamide is the protocol-preferred approach. Procainamide infusion at 20–50 mg/min is initiated with continuous monitoring of QRS width, BP, and rhythm. Endpoints are: arrhythmia suppression, hypotension, QRS widening > 50%, or max dose 17 mg/kg.
phases.triggerCondition: Pre-excited AFib recognized on monitor; patient borderline-stable (SBP > 90, mentation intact); procainamide selected per protocol over AV nodal blockers.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Rapid, irregular pulse; pale, cool, less diaphoretic
otherFindings: 12-lead: irregularly irregular, wide QRS (>120 ms) with varying morphology and visible delta-wave hint
No chest pain, mentation intact, SBP marginal at 92
Defib pads on, sync mode pre-armed in case of deterioration
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: It still feels really fast. Is that medicine going to slow it down?

expectedActions.shortAction: Withhold adenosine, diltiazem, metoprolol, digoxin (pre-excited AFib)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Patient Safety #4 + Notes #3 — avoid AV nodal blocking agents in pre-excited AFib (WPW).
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: With irregular wide complex tachycardia (A-FIB with aberrancy such as Wolff-Parkinson-White and Lown-Ganong Levine), avoid use of AV nodal blocking agents (e.g., adenosine, calcium channel blockers, beta-blockers). Notes/Educational Pearls: Blocking the AV node in some of these patients may lead to impulses that are transmitted exclusively down the accessory pathway, which can result in ventricular fibrillation.

expectedActions.shortAction: Procainamide IV at 20–50 mg/min (max 17 mg/kg = ~1394 mg for 82 kg); stop at suppression / hypotension / QRS widens > 50%
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Irregular Wide Complex Stable step 1 — procainamide is the first-line pharmacologic agent for pre-excited AFib; explicit endpoints required.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Irregular Wide Complex Tachycardia – Stable (A-FIB with aberrancy, pre-excited A-FIB, multifocal atrial tachycardia (MAT) or polymorphic VT/torsades de pointes): Procainamide 20–50 mg/min until arrhythmia suppressed, hypotension ensues, QRS duration increases greater than 50%, or maximum dose 17 mg/kg given. Maintenance infusion: 1–4 mg/min. Avoid if prolonged QT or CHF.

expectedActions.shortAction: If procainamide unsuccessful, amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 min (NOT before procainamide in WPW)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Notes #3 + Patient Safety #5 — amiodarone is acceptable but must follow procainamide in WPW patients.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 minutes. May repeat once as needed. Administration of amiodarone, if needed, should follow procainamide in patients with Wolff–Parkinson–White syndrome. Patient Safety Considerations: Patients with Wolff–Parkinson–White should be given procainamide prior to amiodarone.

expectedActions.shortAction: Sync mode pre-armed; energy 120–150 J biphasic ready if patient deteriorates
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Irregular Wide Unstable + Notes #6 — synchronized cardioversion at biphasic energies if patient becomes unstable.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Irregular Wide Complex Tachycardia – Unstable: Deliver a synchronized shock based on manufacturer's recommendation. For responsive patients, consider sedation. Biphasic waveforms have been proven to convert A-FIB at lower energies and higher rates of success than monophasic waveforms. Strategies include dose escalation (70, 120, 150, 170 joules (J) for biphasic or 100, 200, 300, 360 J for monophasic) versus beginning with single high energy/highest success rate for single shock delivered.

expectedActions.shortAction: Use only one antidysrhythmic at a time; document each push
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Patient Safety #1 — only one antidysrhythmic at a time.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Only use one antidysrhythmic at a time.

transitions.condition: Procainamide infusion successfully suppresses pre-excited AFib; rhythm converts to sinus with WPW pattern visible at baseline; transport with continuous monitoring.

phases.name: Converted to sinus; WPW visible at baseline
phases.description: After approximately 600–800 mg of procainamide infused at 30 mg/min, the rhythm converts to sinus with a short PR interval and a small but unmistakable delta wave — the classic WPW baseline. HR settles in the 80s with normal blood pressure. Crew transitions to maintenance infusion 1–4 mg/min, transports for cardiology evaluation and likely electrophysiology referral, and provides bedside handoff with the conversion strip.
phases.triggerCondition: Procainamide infusion successfully suppressed the pre-excited AFib; baseline WPW conduction now visible; transport for definitive electrophysiology evaluation.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Strong, regular radial pulse; warm skin; cap refill < 2 sec
otherFindings: Rhythm converted to sinus with short PR and delta wave
Patient describes 'I can feel it slowed down — that was wild'
Repeat 12-lead documents WPW pattern at baseline
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: Wow, I can feel it slowed down. I really thought I was going to die.

expectedActions.shortAction: Procainamide maintenance 1–4 mg/min IV; reassess BP and QRS each interval
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Irregular Wide Stable — maintenance infusion 1–4 mg/min.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Procainamide 20–50 mg/min until arrhythmia suppressed, hypotension ensues, QRS duration increases greater than 50%, or maximum dose 17 mg/kg given. Maintenance infusion: 1–4 mg/min. Avoid if prolonged QT or CHF.

expectedActions.shortAction: Save initial, mid-treatment, and post-conversion strips; document times and totals
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Documentation — initial rhythm, all changes, time/dose/response of meds, monitor strips after each intervention.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Documentation Elements: Initial rhythm and all rhythm changes; Time, dose, and response to medications given; Cardioversion times, synchronization, attempts, joules, and response; Obtain monitor strips after each intervention; Patient weight.

expectedActions.shortAction: Transport to ED with cardiology / EP capability; bedside handoff with strips
expectedActions.rationale: Transport and destination decisions are part of the broader care plan; WPW patient with documented pre-excited AFib needs definitive electrophysiology evaluation.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Care Goals: Maintain adequate oxygenation, ventilation, and perfusion. Control ventricular rate. Restore regular sinus rhythm in unstable patient. Search for underlying cause.

phases.name: Adenosine given → VFib arrest
phases.description: Crew misread the irregular wide complex tachycardia as a regular SVT and gave adenosine 6 mg IV. Within seconds the AV node was blocked and conduction was forced exclusively down the accessory pathway, the ventricular rate accelerated to >300, and the rhythm degenerated into ventricular fibrillation. Patient is now pulseless. Immediate high-quality CPR and defibrillation are required.
phases.triggerCondition: AV nodal blocker (adenosine, diltiazem, metoprolol, digoxin) administered to a patient with pre-excited AFib; rhythm degenerated to ventricular fibrillation.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Requires assistance — head positioning and BVM
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Agonal then apneic within 30 seconds of arrest
otherFindings: VFib on monitor within 15-30 seconds of adenosine push
Defib pads now used for therapy, not just monitoring
This is the classic teaching — AV nodal blockade in pre-excited AFib

expectedActions.shortAction: Confirm pulselessness; immediate high-quality CPR
expectedActions.rationale: Cardiac arrest from VFib — immediate CPR is foundational; this is now a Cardiac Arrest call, transitioning out of Tachycardia management.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Care Goals: Maintain adequate oxygenation, ventilation, and perfusion. Patient Safety Considerations: With irregular wide complex tachycardia (A-FIB with aberrancy such as Wolff-Parkinson-White and Lown-Ganong Levine), avoid use of AV nodal blocking agents (e.g., adenosine, calcium channel blockers, beta-blockers). Notes: Blocking the AV node in some of these patients may lead to impulses that are transmitted exclusively down the accessory pathway, which can result in ventricular fibrillation.

expectedActions.shortAction: Defibrillate 200 J biphasic (manufacturer recommendation); resume CPR immediately
expectedActions.rationale: VFib — defibrillation is the only rhythm-converting intervention; per local protocols, follow Cardiac Arrest guideline.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Irregular Wide Complex Tachycardia – Unstable: Deliver a synchronized shock based on manufacturer's recommendation.

expectedActions.shortAction: Recognize iatrogenic VFib; document AV nodal blocker as cause
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Notes #3 — AV nodal blockade in pre-excited AFib can cause VFib; documentation important for QI and handoff.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Avoid AV nodal blocking agents such as adenosine, calcium channel blockers, digoxin, and possibly beta-blockers in patients with pre-excitation A-FIB (e.g., Wolff-Parkinson-White Syndrome, Lown-Ganong-Levine Syndrome) because these drugs may cause a paradoxical increase in the ventricular response. Blocking the AV node in some of these patients may lead to impulses that are transmitted exclusively down the accessory pathway, which can result in ventricular fibrillation.

phases.name: Unstable → synchronized cardioversion
phases.description: Patient deteriorates with worsening hypotension (SBP < 80), AMS, and chest pain before pharmacology can take effect. The pre-excited AFib is now an unstable irregular wide complex tachycardia. Crew correctly transitions to synchronized cardioversion at biphasic 120–150 J with sedation as feasible. This is a valid alternative path that does not punish the crew for selecting cardioversion when the clinical condition warrants it.
phases.triggerCondition: Patient becomes unstable (hypotension, AMS, ischemic chest pain) with the pre-excited AFib still present; synchronized cardioversion indicated per Irregular Wide Complex Unstable algorithm.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Rapid irregular weak central pulse; cool/clammy; cap refill 4 sec
otherFindings: Defib in sync mode at biphasic energy
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: I'm so dizzy. My chest hurts now too. I think I'm going out.

expectedActions.shortAction: Synchronized cardioversion 120–150 J biphasic (manufacturer recommendation)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Irregular Wide Complex Unstable step 1 — synchronized shock per manufacturer.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Irregular Wide Complex Tachycardia – Unstable: Deliver a synchronized shock based on manufacturer's recommendation. For responsive patients, consider sedation. Biphasic waveforms have been proven to convert A-FIB at lower energies and higher rates of success than monophasic waveforms.

expectedActions.shortAction: If responsive: pre-shock sedation/analgesia (e.g., midazolam, fentanyl) per pain mgmt
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Patient Safety #3 + Irregular Wide Unstable step 2 — sedation/pain control if patient responsive prior to cardioversion.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: For responsive patients, consider sedation. Patient Safety Considerations: If using cardioversion, consider sedation and pain control.

expectedActions.shortAction: Reassess rhythm and pulse after each shock; escalate energy if not converted
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Notes #6 — energy escalation strategy for biphasic AFib cardioversion.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Biphasic waveforms have been proven to convert A-FIB at lower energies and higher rates of success than monophasic waveforms. Strategies include dose escalation (70, 120, 150, 170 joules (J) for biphasic or 100, 200, 300, 360 J for monophasic) versus beginning with single high energy/highest success rate for single shock delivered.
